topic: Someone receives an apology letter years after the incident
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope was thick, cream-colored, sealed with a wax stamp that looked impossibly old. Elias stared at it on his cluttered desk, the afternoon sun catching the slight imperfection in the paper. It was from Marcus, a name he hadn't thought about in nearly a decade. His hands began to tremble, the cheap plastic pen slipping from his grasp and clattering onto the wood. He reread the opening line, the ink blurring slightly as he focused. *“Please accept this belated expression of my profound regret.”* A cold knot tightened in his chest, pulling at his ribs. He pushed back from the desk, his chair scraping loudly against the floor. He needed to breathe, but the air felt suddenly too thin. He walked to the window, peering out at the mundane street, unable to settle his gaze on anything solid.

[story 2]

When the mail arrived, Sarah felt the familiar jolt of tension shoot through her. She picked up the letter, her fingers tracing the crisp edges of the stationery. The handwriting was elegant, precise—Marcus’s handwriting. Her breath hitched, catching somewhere in her throat. She unfolded the paper slowly, her eyes scanning the carefully chosen words. Each sentence seemed to land like a small stone in a still pond, sending ripples of disquiet outward. A flush crept up her neck, warm and insistent. She dropped the letter onto the sofa cushion, gripping the fabric until her knuckles turned white. She didn't move for a long time, just listening to the silence of her apartment, the sound of her own rapid heartbeat echoing in the quiet room.

[story 3]

The notification pinged on David’s phone, a mundane email notification, but the subject line made his stomach clench. It was from an unknown sender, but the opening paragraph immediately brought back the memory of that disastrous evening. David sat bolt upright in his worn armchair, his jaw tight. He picked up the printout, his eyes darting over the text, searching for any flaw, any hint of insincerity. His breathing grew shallow, quick and uneven. He reread the apology twice, his gaze fixed on the familiar signature at the bottom. A sudden, sharp need to move—to run, to change the scenery—overtook him. He shoved the paper into his pocket, his entire body vibrating with an internal pressure he couldn't quite dissipate.
